extra.message:
CSRF protection disabled in application middleware stack (csrf_middleware commented out)

The application registers POST routes but does not enable CSRF protection because csrf_middleware is commented out. This allows cross-site request forgery against any state-changing POST endpoint using the victim's session cookies.

Impact: Attackers can force authenticated users to perform unwanted actions (e.g., create/modify reviews/evaluations, logout) by embedding malicious forms/requests on third-party sites.

Fix: Enable CSRF middleware globally (uncomment and ensure it runs before handlers) and/or use a robust CSRF library. Ensure tokens are per-session/per-request and validated for all unsafe methods (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE).

SQL injection via string interpolation in Student.create()

The INSERT statement is built by interpolating the untrusted `name` value directly into the SQL string using Python `%` formatting, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary SQL (e.g., break out of the quoted string and append additional statements).

Impact: Arbitrary SQL execution: data exfiltration/modification, authentication bypass, privilege escalation, or database destruction depending on DB permissions.

Fix: Use parameterized queries: `await cur.execute('INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)', (name,))` (or `%(name)s` with a params dict) and never format user input into SQL strings.

Session cookie HttpOnly flag explicitly disabled (client-side script can read session cookie)

RedisStorage is configured with httponly=False, causing the session cookie to be accessible to JavaScript. Any XSS (and this app also disables template autoescaping) can directly steal session cookies.

Impact: Session hijacking via XSS or malicious third-party scripts; compromise of authenticated accounts and access to PII.

Fix: Set httponly=True for session cookies. Also set Secure and SameSite attributes where supported (e.g., secure=True in production, samesite='Lax' or 'Strict').

Stored XSS via unescaped auth_user name fields in base template navbar

auth_user.first_name/middle_name/last_name are rendered without escaping. If these fields are user-controlled (e.g., profile data), they can inject script into every page for logged-in users when autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Persistent XSS across the entire authenticated UI; can hijack sessions and perform actions as the user.

Fix: Escape all name fields (|e) and enable autoescape globally; validate/sanitize profile fields.

Stored XSS via unescaped course fields and review text on course detail page

Course title/description and review.review_text are rendered directly. These values are user-controlled (course created via POST; reviews created via POST) and, with autoescape disabled, allow stored XSS.

Impact: Persistent XSS affecting all users viewing the course page; can steal sessions/CSRF tokens or perform actions as the victim.

Fix: Turn on autoescape; escape these variables with |e; consider HTML sanitization if rich text is desired.

Stored XSS via unescaped course title rendering in courses list

Course fields (title/description) originate from user-controlled POST in views.courses and are rendered without explicit escaping. With Jinja2 autoescape disabled in this app context, this becomes a stored XSS sink.

Impact: Attacker can persist JavaScript in course title/description and execute it in any visitor’s browser, enabling session theft and account takeover.

Fix: Enable Jinja2 autoescaping globally; additionally apply |e to all user-controlled fields (course.title, course.description) and validate/sanitize input on creation.

Reflected/stored XSS via unescaped course title in review form header

The review form displays course.title without escaping in the card title. If course.title contains HTML/JS, it will execute (stored XSS).

Impact: Stored XSS triggers when any user opens the review form for a maliciously named course.

Fix: Escape course.title (|e) and enable autoescape globally.

Stored XSS via unescaped course fields in student results view

course.title and course.description are rendered without escaping in the student detail page. These fields are user-controlled and become stored XSS sinks when autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Persistent XSS affecting users viewing student pages that include the malicious course.

Fix: Escape course.title/course.description (|e) and enable autoescape globally.

Stored XSS via unescaped student name in students list

Student names are created from POST data and rendered in the students list without escaping. With autoescape disabled, this is a stored XSS sink.

Impact: Persistent XSS affecting any user viewing the students list.

Fix: Enable autoescape; escape name with |e; validate/sanitize student names on input.

Missing authorization check: any user can create students (and unauthenticated if route allows)

students() allows POST to create students with no authentication/authorization checks. Template shows the form only when auth_user is set, but server-side enforcement is absent.

Impact: Unauthorized creation of records; can be used to inject stored XSS payloads and pollute data.

Fix: Enforce authentication and role/permission checks in the handler for POST; do not rely on template/UI gating.

Redis container likely running without authentication/ACLs

The docker-compose configuration uses the default redis:alpine image with no command/config to require a password or enable ACLs. Default Redis instances accept unauthenticated connections on the network.

Impact: Attackers with network access to the Redis service can read/alter session data and cached data, potentially hijacking sessions or disrupting service.

Fix: Configure Redis with requirepass/ACLs, bind to localhost/private network, and do not expose Redis ports publicly. Use Docker networks and firewall rules to restrict access.

Jinja2 autoescape disabled globally (amplifies session theft and CSRF via XSS)

Jinja2 is configured with autoescape=False, disabling HTML escaping across templates by default. This makes reflected/stored XSS far more likely wherever user-controlled data is rendered.

Impact: XSS can be used to steal non-HttpOnly cookies (and HttpOnly-disabled session cookies here), perform actions as the user, and exfiltrate PII.

Fix: Enable autoescape (autoescape=True or select_autoescape for HTML/XML). Audit templates and explicitly mark safe content only when necessary.

Weak password hashing using unsalted MD5

Passwords are verified using a raw MD5 hash with no salt or work factor. MD5 is fast and broken for password storage, enabling efficient offline cracking and rainbow-table attacks if hashes are leaked.

Impact: Compromise of user passwords (including password reuse across services), account takeover, and potential admin compromise.

Fix: Use a modern password hashing algorithm with salt and cost (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt, or PBKDF2 with strong parameters). Store per-user salts and migrate hashes on next login.

Missing Secure flag for session cookies (no TLS-only cookie enforcement)

Session storage/cookie configuration does not set the Secure attribute. Without Secure, browsers may send session cookies over plaintext HTTP, enabling interception on untrusted networks.

Impact: Session hijacking via network sniffing/mitm when users access the site over HTTP or mixed-content scenarios.

Fix: Configure session cookies with secure=True (TLS-only) and deploy behind HTTPS. If terminating TLS at a proxy, ensure the app/proxy sets Secure cookies correctly.

Verbose error objects passed to templates may disclose internal details

The error handlers render templates with the full exception object in the context ({'error': exc}). If templates display exception messages/attributes, this can leak stack traces, SQL errors, internal URLs, or other sensitive details to clients.

Impact: Information disclosure that can reveal implementation details and facilitate targeted attacks (e.g., SQLi payload tuning, path discovery).

Fix: Render generic user-facing error pages. Log detailed exceptions server-side only. If passing error info to templates, pass only a safe error code/message and never raw exception objects.

CSRF token missing from evaluate form in course template

The evaluate form posts to a state-changing endpoint but does not include a hidden CSRF token field, making CSRF trivial even if validation were later added elsewhere.

Impact: Cross-site requests can submit evaluations/grades.

Fix: Add `<input type="hidden" name="_csrf_token" value="{{ csrf_token() }}">` to the form and validate server-side.

Information exposure / potential XSS sink by rendering error.__dict__ in 50x template

The 50x error template renders error.__dict__ directly. This can expose internal details and may include attacker-controlled strings (e.g., exception messages containing user input), which under autoescape disabled can lead to reflected XSS.

Impact: Leak of internal state/stack context and possible reflected XSS on server error pages.

Fix: Remove rendering of internal exception objects; show a generic error message; ensure autoescape is enabled.

Sensitive data in template context: full auth_user object injected into templates

auth_user_processor injects the full auth_user model object into the template context. Model objects often contain sensitive fields (password hash, roles, internal IDs, tokens). Even if templates don't render them now, this crosses a trust boundary into rendering context.

Impact: Increases risk of accidental sensitive data exposure in templates and via template debugging; may expose password hashes/tokens if referenced.

Fix: Inject only a safe subset (e.g., id, display_name, is_admin) or a dedicated view model/dict without sensitive fields.

CSRF token generation exists but no server-side enforcement across handlers (frontend-only CSRF)

csrf_processor generates a token and templates include it, but the reviewed POST handlers do not validate it. This creates a false sense of CSRF protection.

Impact: All state-changing endpoints remain CSRF-vulnerable despite token presence.

Fix: Add CSRF validation middleware that checks _csrf_token for unsafe methods and compares to session; reject missing/invalid tokens.

Review creation POST missing authentication (anyone can post reviews)

review() allows POST to create a review without checking that the caller is authenticated.

Impact: Unauthenticated attackers can create arbitrary reviews, including stored XSS payloads, and spam content.

Fix: Require authentication for posting reviews (or implement anti-abuse controls like CAPTCHA/moderation); enforce authorization rules.

Login endpoint missing rate limiting / brute-force protection

The login logic in index() accepts unlimited POST attempts with no throttling, lockout, or IP/user-based rate limiting.

Impact: Enables credential stuffing and brute-force attacks against user accounts.

Fix: Add rate limiting (per IP and per username), exponential backoff, and/or temporary account lockouts; log and alert on suspicious activity.

Sensitive data in template context: index view passes full auth_user object to template

The index handler returns auth_user directly in the template context. If User contains sensitive fields (password hash, tokens), they become accessible to templates and could be leaked if rendered or via template errors.

Impact: Potential leakage of sensitive user attributes through templates.

Fix: Pass only necessary safe fields (e.g., display name, is_admin) rather than full model objects.

CSRF protection appears missing/unenforced for login POST

The login handler processes POST credentials but does not verify the submitted _csrf_token against the session token. Templates include a hidden _csrf_token, but there is no server-side validation here, indicating CSRF is not actually enforced.

Impact: Cross-site request forgery can force a victim browser to log in as an attacker-controlled account (login CSRF), enabling session confusion and potential data exposure.

Fix: Implement server-side CSRF validation middleware for all state-changing requests; reject requests with missing/invalid CSRF tokens; consider SameSite cookies.

IDOR/data exposure: student detail endpoint accessible without authentication/authorization

The student handler returns a student object and associated marks/results for any student_id in the URL with no authentication/authorization checks. This leaks potentially sensitive educational records/PII.

Impact: Any user can enumerate student IDs and view names and marks/grades.

Fix: Require authentication and enforce per-user authorization (only admins/teachers or the student themselves). Consider using non-guessable identifiers if appropriate.

Missing input validation: course creation does not use COURSE_SCHEMA (allows XSS payloads/oversized input)

Course.create is called with raw POST title/description without schema validation. Allows storing script payloads and very large strings.

Impact: Stored XSS and potential resource exhaustion via huge descriptions.

Fix: Validate with COURSE_SCHEMA; enforce length limits; sanitize/escape output.

Logging configured at DEBUG level globally

The application sets logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG). Debug logs can include sensitive data (PII, tokens, SQL errors) depending on other log statements and library behavior.

Impact: Sensitive information may be written to logs and later accessed by attackers or leaked via log aggregation systems.

Fix: Default to INFO/WARN in production; make log level configurable per environment. Scrub sensitive fields from logs.

Application debug mode enabled (debug=True) in web app configuration

The aiohttp Application is created with debug=True. Debug mode can expose verbose error details, stack traces, and other sensitive runtime information depending on framework configuration.

Impact: Information disclosure that can aid exploitation (paths, configuration, code structure); may change error handling behavior in production.

Fix: Disable debug in production (debug=False) and control via environment/config. Ensure production error pages are generic.

Sensitive password hash field included in User object returned from DAO (risk of accidental exposure to templates/logs)

User.get selects and returns the pwd_hash field as part of the User NamedTuple. This increases the risk of password hashes crossing trust boundaries (e.g., being passed into template contexts, serialized in responses, or logged) even when not needed for most operations.

Impact: Accidental disclosure of password hashes via rendering, debugging, or logging; increases blast radius of other info-disclosure bugs.

Fix: Avoid returning pwd_hash except where strictly required (e.g., authentication). Create separate DAO methods/DTOs for public user profile vs auth record, or explicitly omit pwd_hash in general-purpose getters.

Redis configuration schema lacks password/TLS fields (encourages unauthenticated Redis and potential data exposure)

The Redis config schema includes only host/port/db and provides no way to configure Redis AUTH (password) or TLS. This can lead to deployments with unauthenticated Redis, exposing session data and other cached sensitive data if Redis is reachable.

Impact: If Redis is exposed on the network, attackers may read/modify session data, leading to account takeover and PII exposure.

Fix: Add redis password (and TLS options if applicable) to the schema and enforce authentication in Redis. Ensure Redis is network-restricted.

Potential cleartext DB connection (no TLS/sslmode enforced)

The PostgreSQL DSN does not specify TLS requirements (e.g., `sslmode=require` / SSL context). In many deployments this results in cleartext transmission of credentials and data over the network.

Impact: Network attackers could sniff DB credentials/PII or tamper with DB traffic if the DB is not strictly local/secured.

Fix: Enforce TLS by configuring `sslmode=require` (or stronger) and providing an SSL context/CA verification as appropriate; ensure DB is not reachable over untrusted networks.

Redis connection without authentication/TLS (potential cleartext and unauthorized access)

The Redis pool is created using only host/port/db with no password/ACL and no TLS configuration. If Redis is reachable beyond localhost or within a shared network, attackers may connect, read/modify session/cache data, or perform privilege escalation via session manipulation.

Impact: Session/cache compromise, data exfiltration, and potential authentication bypass if sessions or auth state are stored in Redis.

Fix: Require Redis AUTH/ACLs and enable TLS (rediss:// or SSL context) where supported; bind Redis to localhost/private network; enforce firewall rules; store credentials securely.

Reflected XSS via unescaped validation errors in evaluate error page

Validation errors are rendered directly. While current schema errors are typically server-generated, they may include attacker-controlled strings in some cases (e.g., malformed inputs echoed in error messages) and become XSS sinks with autoescape disabled.

Impact: Potential reflected XSS on error pages if error content includes user input.

Fix: Enable autoescape; escape fieldname/errors; avoid including raw user input in error messages.

Reflected XSS via unescaped last_visited session value on index page

last_visited is read from session and rendered without escaping. If an attacker can set/poison this session value (e.g., via session fixation or other bug), it becomes an XSS sink under autoescape=False.

Impact: Potential XSS on the landing page for a victim whose session value is attacker-controlled.

Fix: Enable autoescape; escape last_visited; ensure session values cannot be attacker-controlled and regenerate session on login.

Evaluate endpoint missing rate limiting

No throttling on evaluation submissions.

Impact: Automated abuse can create many marks, polluting data and consuming resources.

Fix: Add per-user/IP rate limiting and audit logging for grade changes.

Session fixation risk: session ID not regenerated on successful login

On successful authentication, the handler sets session['user_id'] but does not regenerate/rotate the session identifier.

Impact: If an attacker can set or obtain a victim’s pre-auth session ID, they may be able to reuse it after login to hijack the authenticated session.

Fix: Regenerate/rotate the session ID upon successful login (framework-specific session renewal) and consider clearing other session data.

Potential SQL injection/unsafe query construction pattern in get_many() LIMIT/OFFSET concatenation (Course)

The query string is dynamically concatenated with LIMIT/OFFSET clauses. Although values are passed as parameters, dynamic SQL construction is an unsafe pattern and can become injectable if future changes interpolate values directly or if the driver parameterization is misused. Additionally, the `LIMIT + %(limit)s` / `OFFSET + %(offset)s` syntax is unusual and may lead developers to 'fix' it by string formatting later.

Impact: Currently likely limited, but increases risk of future SQL injection regressions and may cause query errors that lead to insecure hotfixes.

Fix: Keep SQL static and use correct parameter placeholders without concatenation; validate `limit/offset` as bounded integers; use `await cur.execute(q, params)` with a dict consistently.

Incorrect parameter binding in Student.get_many() may lead to query manipulation or runtime errors (limit/offset handling)

Student.get_many() uses `LIMIT + %(limit)s` / `OFFSET + %(offset)s` and passes a dict as the second argument. The `+` tokens and placeholder style are error-prone and may cause runtime errors or encourage unsafe string formatting changes later.

Impact: Potential DoS via repeated triggering of failing queries; future maintenance risk of introducing SQL injection.

Fix: Use standard parameterized SQL with validated integer bounds: `... LIMIT %s OFFSET %s` and pass a tuple, or use psycopg2 named placeholders correctly without extra operators.

Reflected XSS: unescaped error.status rendered in 40x error title

Error status is rendered without escaping in the title. Usually numeric and safe, but if error object is attacker-influenced or mis-typed and autoescape is disabled, could be an XSS sink.

Impact: Potential XSS in error pages under type confusion.

Fix: Ensure status is int; enable autoescape; optionally apply |e.

Template bug may leak incorrect error variable in review form (could expose unexpected data)

Loop uses `for fieldname, errors in errors.items` but prints `{{ fieldname }}: {{ error }}` (undefined `error`). Depending on template engine behavior, this could render a global `error` object or raise an exception that triggers verbose 50x page (which leaks error.__dict__).

Impact: May trigger 500 errors and leak internals via 50x template; or display unintended variable contents.

Fix: Fix variable name (iterate over error list properly) and ensure production error pages do not expose internals.
